name: Flying Geese Table Runner
description: A flock of flying geese marching across your table. The no-waste flying geese method makes this faster than you'd expect.
summary: Create 24 flying geese units using the no-waste method (4-at-a-time), arrange in three columns with sashing, add borders, and finish into an elegant table runner.

steps.title: Mark Background Squares
steps.instruction: Draw a diagonal line from corner to corner on the wrong side of all 24 background squares (2 7/8"). Use a fine pencil or Frixion pen.

tips.text: Frixion pens disappear with heat from your iron — no visible marks on the finished quilt.

steps.title: No-Waste Flying Geese — First Seam
steps.instruction: Place 2 background squares on opposite corners of one large goose square, RST (Right Sides Together). The drawn lines should form a continuous diagonal across the large square. Sew 1/4" on BOTH sides of the drawn line.

warnings.message: Sew on BOTH sides of the line. This is what makes the no-waste method work — you get 4 geese from each large square.

tips.text: Chain-piece all 6 sets at once for efficiency.

steps.title: Cut and Press First Pair
steps.instruction: Cut ON the drawn line to separate into two units. Press each unit open — the background triangles press away from the goose. You now have 2 heart-shaped units.

tips.text: Press from the front to make sure the seam is fully open and flat.

steps.title: No-Waste Flying Geese — Second Seam
steps.instruction: Place one background square on each heart-shaped unit, RST, with the drawn line running from the center point to the outer corner. Sew 1/4" on both sides of the drawn line. Cut on the line, press open.

warnings.message: Make sure the diagonal line runs from the point where the first two triangles meet to the opposite corner.

steps.title: Trim Flying Geese
steps.instruction: Trim all 24 flying geese units to 2 1/2" x 4 1/2". The goose point should be 1/4" from the top edge, and background corners should be 1/4" from each side.

warnings.message: Don't cut off the goose points! Leave a full 1/4" seam allowance above each point.

tips.text: A dedicated flying geese ruler makes trimming much faster and more accurate.

steps.title: Arrange Columns
steps.instruction: Arrange flying geese into 3 columns of 8, all pointing the same direction (toward one end of the runner). Lay them out on your table to preview the finished look.

tips.text: All geese pointing the same way creates movement. Try alternating for a different look.

steps.title: Sew Columns
steps.instruction: Sew each column of 8 geese together, matching points carefully. Press all seams in the same direction within each column. Each column should measure 4 1/2" x 48 1/2".

warnings.message: Pin at each goose point intersection. The points are the star of this pattern — don't lose them in the seam!

steps.title: Add Sashing Between Columns
steps.instruction: Sew the 1 1/2" sashing strips between the three columns. Press seams toward the sashing.

tips.text: Pin sashing at every geese seam junction to keep everything aligned.

steps.title: Add Borders
steps.instruction: Sew long border strips to the sides, then short strips to the ends. Press seams toward borders. Your runner top should now measure 16 1/2" x 52 1/2".

tips.text: Measure through the center, not along the edges, for accurate border lengths.

steps.title: Quilt and Bind
steps.instruction: Layer backing, batting, and runner top. Baste, then quilt — straight lines 1/4" from each seam echo the geese beautifully. Trim, bind with 2 1/2" strips using your preferred method.

tips.text: Walking foot quilting with straight lines is the easiest finish for this pattern.
Spray basting is faster than pin basting for small projects like runners.
